range.nativeRange: Northern China and Japan; naturalized in parts of Europe and North America

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US — introduced from East Asia. Occasionally naturalized in scattered locations but not widespread in the wild

invasive.description: Not listed as invasive or noxious in any US state. Spreads vigorously by tubers in cultivated settings and can be difficult to eradicate once established, but does not naturalize aggressively into wild habitats. Not on any federal or state noxious weed list. The spreading concern is garden-scale persistence rather than ecological invasion

description.spread: 12–18 inches per plant; spreads indefinitely by tubers if not contained
description.growthRate: Moderate; spreads more aggressively after first establishment year
description.timeToMaturity: First tuber harvest in fall of planting year
description.lifespan: Perennial; effectively permanent once established due to persistent tubers
description.deciduousEvergreen: Deciduous — tops die back after frost

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun to partial shade; shade-tolerant enough for food forest understory
lightAndWater.water: Moderate; consistent moisture preferred, especially while tubers are sizing up in late summer. Tolerates brief dry spells once established

drainagePreference.description: Moist but well-drained; does not tolerate waterlogged soil. Standing water or saturated conditions cause tuber rot. Needs soil that drains freely after rain — raised beds or mounded rows are strongly preferred in any heavy-soil site

claySoilPerformance.description: Poor — heavy clay causes two problems: waterlogging leads to tuber rot, and compaction makes tubers difficult to develop properly and nearly impossible to harvest cleanly. In clay soils, deep amendment with compost and coarse organic matter is essential, or grow in raised beds filled with loose, well-drained mix. Unamended clay is not viable for this crop

soil.texturePreference: Loose, friable, well-amended loam; loose soil is critical for tuber development and clean harvest. Sandy loam is ideal. Heavy clay or compacted soil must be amended before planting — tubers form in the top 6 inches and need soil they can push through
soil.rootCharacter: Shallow fibrous roots with stolons terminating in small spiral tubers; tubers typically 1–2 inches long and form in the top 6 inches of soil

bloom.bloomDescription: Small, tubular two-lipped flowers in whorls up the stem; pale pink to lavender; typical mint family appearance; not showy
bloom.fragrance: Mild; foliage has faint minty-herbaceous scent when bruised
bloom.pollination: Self-fertile; also visited by bees and small pollinators

yield.edibleParts: Tubers (the primary crop); small, segmented, spiral-shaped; eaten raw, steamed, roasted, sautéed, pickled, or added to stir-fries; flavor is nutty, mildly sweet, similar to water chestnut or sunchoke but milder; texture remains crisp even when cooked
yield.harvestSeason: Fall — after first frost kills back the tops; tubers are at full size and sweetest after a frost. Harvest by digging carefully; almost impossible to get every tuber, so plant will persist
yield.medicinalUses: Minor traditional uses in Chinese medicine; tubers are high in stachyose (a prebiotic oligosaccharide); not a primary medicinal plant

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: None; fully edible. Note: stachyose content may cause gas in some individuals (similar to sunchoke effect), though typically milder
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: None known; tubers are generally considered safe
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: None documented; typical mint family spreading behavior is mechanical (tubers), not chemical

roles: Edible ground cover; living mulch; rhizosphere crop; food forest understory plant; tuber yield; minor pollinator support (late-season bloom)
companionPlants: mulberry (works as edible ground layer under all three guilds); comfrey (tolerate each other well; comfrey's deep roots won't compete with shallow tubers); garlic; other shade-tolerant herbs. Partners well with taller guild plants that leave ground-level light available
antagonists: No well-documented antagonists; avoid planting where soil is too wet or compacted for tuber development

wildlife.attracts: Bees and small pollinators visiting late-season flowers; minimal wildlife value compared to fruiting plants
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Not documented as a significant host
wildlife.nestingHabitat: Dense low foliage may provide minor ground-level cover

deer.description: Moderate — deer may browse foliage; mint family plants are sometimes avoided but not reliably so. Tubers underground are protected from browsing. Young spring growth is most vulnerable. In high-pressure areas, temporary fencing or repellent sprays may be needed until plants establish a dense patch

salt.description: Low — not salt-tolerant; avoid planting near salted roads or walkways

drought.description: Low to moderate — needs consistent moisture for good tuber yield; not a drought plant. In arid or hot-summer regions, mulch heavily and irrigate regularly during the tuber-sizing period (late summer)

tolerances.other: Shade-tolerant (a key asset for food forest understory use); frost-tolerant as a perennial root crop (tops die, tubers survive underground through winter); does not tolerate waterlogged soil or heavy compaction

maintenance.maintenanceLevel: Low to moderate — easy to grow, but spreading must be managed; can be contained with root barriers (similar to mint) or by thorough annual harvest
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back dead tops after frost; no other pruning required
propagationMethods: Tubers — save a portion of the harvest and replant in spring
2–4 inches deep; can also be ordered from specialty seed and root suppliers. Division is simple and reliable

cultivarNotes: Very few named cultivars exist; typically sold as the straight species (Stachys affinis). Some sources list a 'White' tuber form, referring to the creamy-white tuber color, but this is not a meaningfully distinct cultivar — all commercially available stock is essentially the species. Performance is consistent across Zones 5–9 with no regional cultivar selection needed. Source from reputable specialty food plant suppliers (Strictly Medicinal Seeds, Horizon Herbs, or similar) rather than general nurseries, which rarely carry this plant.

suggestedUse: Edible ground layer in food forest guilds; specialty root vegetable in vegetable or kitchen garden; novelty crop with high culinary interest for adventurous gardeners
gardenUse.designPlacement: Best grown as a food forest ground layer under deciduous fruit or nut trees, or in dedicated raised beds for vegetable production. Shade-tolerant — suitable for understory placement where other root crops would fail. Small tubers are harvested in fall after frost. Spreads by tubers and can become difficult to remove; contain with buried root barriers or harvest thoroughly each fall. Keep away from beds where spreading would be problematic. In loose, well-drained soil with consistent moisture, it is a low-maintenance edible ground cover
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: Not yet acquired — specialty sourcing required; not typically available at local nurseries

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: No — introduced from East Asia; not native to Pennsylvania or any part of the eastern US
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not regulated in Pennsylvania. Not on the PA Dept. of Agriculture invasive species list or DCNR watch list. Same as general Invasive field — not a regulatory concern
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: The red clay at Hegins is a poor match for in-ground planting without significant preparation. Raised beds or heavily amended planting pockets are required — loose soil to at least 8 inches depth. Compost and leaf mold worked into the clay will improve both drainage and harvestability. The vegetable garden raised beds remove this constraint entirely
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins red clay requires deep amendment with compost before in-ground planting. Raised vegetable beds with imported soil mix are the simplest path. If planting under tree guilds, prepare individual planting pockets with loosened, amended soil rather than attempting to grow directly in unamended clay
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: the straight species is the only option and performs well in this zone; source from specialty suppliers and plan for spring planting after last frost.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Good candidate for the understory of the apple or paw paw guilds, functioning as an edible ground layer under the canopy. Also suitable for raised vegetable beds where soil conditions are already ideal. If planting under tree guilds in ground, prepare amended planting pockets in the clay
pennsylvaniaHegins.siteObservations: Amend planting area with compost to loosen the red clay before any in-ground planting — loose soil is essential for both tuber development and clean harvest. Consider planting within a buried root barrier (like mint) or in a dedicated raised section to prevent spreading into adjacent plantings. Deer pressure from the east corridor is a moderate concern for foliage browsing but tubers are protected underground. The clay drainage issues at this site make raised beds or heavily amended soil a near-requirement for reliable tuber production
